evidence_quotes.quote: I buy a video game and I can no longer play it from that very moment when the publisher closes the servers.
It is highly unfair that as consumers we should pay increasingly higher prices, but get less and less for them.
I buy a film on a video streaming platform and lose access to it after a few weeks, as the film producer no longer wants to sell it on the video Stream platform.

paragraphs.text: In my view, such legislation should include that consumers, after purchasing digital goods (such as: Software or videogames) do not purchase a licence, but belong to you. We see an increasing alienation between the purchase of a product and the actual possession of the product in the digital space. That cannot, in my view, go any further. It is highly unfair that as consumers we should pay increasingly higher prices, but get less and less for them. As consumers of the arbitrariness of the selling companies, we are supplied without protection. And it is precisely this protection that must finally be guaranteed by such a law. See an example: I buy a film on a video streaming platform and lose access to it after a few weeks, as the film producer no longer wants to sell it on the video Stream platform. I was therefore deprived of my possession after a few weeks. According to current statutory requirements, this seems to be legal, but I do not think it is correct and I think that the Digital Fairness Act needs to be expanded for this very purpose. One more example: I buy a video game and I can no longer play it from that very moment when the publisher closes the servers. This is also the case here: I am stolen by arbitrarily by companies. As most video Games now require an internet connection, the full decision-making power lies with the player. I think it is very important that we, as consumers, continue to have access to the software once we have bought it. The Digital Fairness Act must also take this into account. I am afraid of a future where we don’t have anything anymore and the arbitrariness and girre of big, mostly international, companies are unprotected. We must therefore take action now! The Digital Fairness Act must be a first important and right step!
